data.title: Indra Sistemas: The Valuation Is Too High In 2026
data.description:
If you've been an investor in Indra Sistemas (ISMAY) during 2025, chances are it's time for me (and other analysts who did not) to congratulate you. After all, you invested where others did not and where the upside has been rewarding. The company has surged well over 100% this year, moving from a native price of about €16/share to over €57/share. That's a massive return for a company of this size and of these fundamentals. In this article, I will be providing my first coverage for the company, as well as a, from what I can tell by looking at SA valuation and target trends, a counterpoint to what coverage there is. Wall Street doesn't cover the company - Quant coverage is also non-existent, and what coverage does exist focuses on the upside in defense in Europe, and lauds the company as a player with more upside.

I am not saying that these points are invalid at all - what I am saying is that the valuation given to these growth metrics and forecasts, when considering company fundamentals and historicals, do not necessarily make the most sense.

Based on this, I'm happy to "throw my hat in the ring", as it were, and provide my own view on what this company should be worth. This, as indicated, will be my very first coverage of the company.

For US/NA-based investors, this company trades on the MSE, the Madrid stock exchange, under the native ticker IDR, and has, among others, the ADR ISMAY. As usual, my humble suggestion would be to focus on investing not in the ADR, but in the native, where possible, as many brokers now offer native access trading. I suggest this for reasons of liquidity as well as currency diversification.

That being said, let's get going here.

Indra Sistemas S.A - Finding upside in overvaluation

Many are the companies that have seen the way the wind is blowing in Europe for the past few years. Indra Sistemas is one of them. Not a pure-play defense player in any way in the sense that it manufactures munitions or anything of the sort, it's an IT-oriented service and hardware provider. If you look at the company description, it's a generalized overview of details consulting, project, integration, IT outsourcing, and Business processes, or BPO.

This could, of course, describe thousands of businesses in Europe. What makes Indra unique is its exposure to the defense sector, not just in Spain, but other countries.

It works with things like aviation technology, focusing on radar, electronic warfare, simulators, and UAV technology. However, as you might expect for an older company (because after all, it's been around for at least 30 years), it can't have survived that long with just military tech. It's less than €300M net income (which is high for 2024A) isn't enough to justify an upside if that was the case, based on its growth and revenue. Instead, it also has a civil segment, where the company is a major player in ATM technology.

Air traffic management, as the skies are getting more congested, is getting more and more important. And Indra offers a third of the world's ATM systems.

This is the civil upside. To say that I have been unaware of the company is wrong - to say that I didn't expect it to outperform, that on the other hand would be an accurate statement. Aside from the ATM segment, it's military appeal was at best "so-so" before Ukraine and the current geopolitical situation, and it's other segments, such as mobility was more exposed to competition, and it's Minsait, LATAM-focused segment was a bit too unstable for my taste. The fact that it's yield was also less than 1% even at the best of times, saw me consider other companies as far more attractive than Indra.

It's not that Indra is too local - it's operations are found in over 40 countries across the globe, but much of its upside and operations are too ethereal and too forward-looking and uncertain for me to have paid them much serious mind, until the military saw more appeal in 2025.

The company's goals and its earnings have seen me reconsider its overall upside here, however. While it won't be an easy road to get where the company's going, I'm prepared to grant it a bigger-than-previous role in European and potentially international defense roles, backed up by the aforementioned strong civil air traffic management segment. However, the EBIT split above should make it clear to you why I haven't given the company all that much focus before. Even though ATM has been a part of the company for some time, before the military growth, it was mostly a Minsait (which again, I consider volatile and somewhat uncertain) and a smaller ATM segment. Mobility is so small that it almost, at this point, fails to hold much relevance next to defense and Minsait segment results.

So the reason the company is getting more attractive here is the upside in military/defense.

I say this because Minsait has at best been a somewhat speculative "arm" of the company, with the company rumored to be looking to divest it (at least there have been offers from various PE funds) or parts of it, forming an uncertainty for investors. This uncertainty causes market fluctuations, which I given the lack of visibility for both defense and the company's non-ATM-focused segments, have hard a hard time putting a discount or a price on.

The one thing that makes Indra more interesting to me now, and which also differentiates it from your bread-and-butter IT companies in the military, is that it offers end-to-end integrated product solutions. It uses proprietary suites and is able to integrate hardware from third-party solutions as well. And this is also the operations that it's expanding, with things like new radar as well as being part of the FCAS, or Future Combat Air System, which is part of the most advanced current defense project in Europe.

So, Indra in 2026 is certainly more interesting than Indra from about 2-3 years ago.

But, this does not mean that the concentration risk or volatility is over.

As you can see above, the lion's share of revenue concentration and EBITDA breakdown comes from Spain and Minsait, respectively. Minsait, in my view, lacks the competitive advantage of some of the company's defense operations, and sees impact from other IT companies like Accenture (ACN) and CapGemini (CGEMY), both of which I cover here on Seeking Alpha - and those are just the bigger companies, with dozens of smaller ones vying for market space as well.

So whenever a company with that exposure starts trading up 100-300%, one of the first things I do to avoid a crash down is to see what sort of justification we have for that sort of upswing.

In this case, my short answer is some, but not as much as that.

Valuation for Indra Sistemas - Tricky, despite what the market may tell you

Often, a picture will tell you more than words. This is one of those times, even if I am going to be supplementing that picture with clear wording.

The conviction or assumption that a company can completely disentangle itself from its historical trends is one that I've found to be flawed in most companies I cover. What you see, therefore, is a company that over the past 20 years can best be characterized as "troubled", and has gone negative several times, only to see a significant surge related to the current macro situation, and a massive surge in the market this year.

Could it have been in any way forecasted? Not to this level, I argue, no.

Is it justified? I would argue, based on accuracy, that it's not.

In order to understand the negative trends that the company has been seeing, you need to understand the cyclical Spanish economy, which still represents a significant source of earnings. This government is subject to significant spending shifts and political changes, and overall political volatility.

This is not a political article, but an economic one. I will, however, say that I consider Spain to be one of the most volatile EU states to invest in - even more so than some Eastern-European geographies, due to significant unresolved historical legacies, fragmentation, and political tensions. Normally, these wouldn't matter all that much when investing, but here you're investing in a company with significant exposure to politics. If you fail to take this into account, it's like investing in a Southern American company without accounting for politics and FX. Spain was one of Western Europe's longest dictatorships (except for part of Germany, the DDR/GDR), which lasted until 1975, when Gerald Ford was president, and the EU was not a new thing. Let me put it clearly - investing in Spain or a Spanish business is not like investing in, say, a German business.

There are differences that need to be realized, accounted for, and priced for. Just because Indra Sistemas is doing splendidly right now does not mean that this will continue unabated, or that the current valuation is justified (the more important point).

Moving from the "political and historical segway" here back to valuation, I consider a discount of at least 30-50% to be valid here, given the underlying volatility, the lack of an IG rating, the lack of a solid dividend track record, the negative earnings history, and other risks. I validate the company's growth forecast of 10-22% in the next 3-4 years due to certain visibility in defense, but even if it were to grow at 18-20% per year, and even if you were to forecast it at 23-24x P/E, you would not be getting market-beating returns if you invest now.

So, even if you were to ignore the political realities I describe (which I believe are relevant), and if you were to claim a forecast to a 60% higher P/E than historically, with these growth forecasts and ignoring the fact that the company misses negatively more than 60% of the time, this would not even give you 15% annualized at current forecasts. And I believe you, as an investor, should want a lot more than that.

I therefore say any upside the company could rightly expect from military and from good recent results is already included in what the company trades at today. I estimate no more than a 15-20% annualized AEPS growth, but based on historical multiples, as well as a necessity (in my view), of discounting what the company offers here by at least 30-40% due to inherent volatility and the lacks described, I would go no higher than a discounted 15-17x P/E, which comes to a fair value of about €37/share, with a PT of €29/share.

Risks for the company are as follows.

Risks & Considerations

The risks for this company are primarily related to competition for its more commoditized sectors, such as Minsait, while the non-commoditized sectors, such as defense/space, are subject to government funding. As I've pointed out in previous articles on defense contractors, while we're currently seeing a very solid trend for many of these companies, the assumption that this trend is going to last forever or even for a long time is a dangerous one.

Like many other companies with a defense exposure or focus, Indra Sistemas trades at a significant premium to any comparative period. Its current 30x P/E is more than 90% higher than the 20-year average of 15-16x P/E. While current forecasts detail a 20-30% annualized AEPS growth rate, those forecasts come with a historical failure forecast rate of well over 50%, and should not, in my view, be taken "as gospel".

If you instead look at the historical trends, I argue that you see a very worrying historical trend as well as a worrying current valuation trend, which speaks of irrationality, not rationality. Rating this company as a "BUY" (or higher), therefore takes part in that irrationality, and unless you believe either that the irrationality is going to continue, or that there is no irrationality (in which case I'd be interested in that conclusion), you're taking a massive risk here.

Based on my current estimates, I give the company a PT of no higher than €29/share and a fair value of €37/share. I don't believe the earnings are going to turn out as rosy as is currently being estimated.

My thesis is as follows.

Thesis

Indra Sistemas is a good IT/Services/hardware business with an eye towards defense that's been rising for several years now. It has surged significantly during 2025, and if you've invested in the company as of late, it's likely that you're now sitting on very significant gains. However, the gains and the positivity hide an underlying volatility that has been characterizing results for the past 20 years.

Furthermore, the company's operating geography is volatile, and even its civil sectors can't completely immunize it from volatility. The valuation surge is serious enough that I would argue that only a significantly discounted price makes sense. That's the price I also set as my introductory share price here in my first coverage of the company.

I believe that you may, and are likely to, outperform the market over time if you buy here, though your timeframe will have to be longer than I might have originally expected. My price target is €29/share, introduced here in early 2026. The ADR ISMAY comes to a comparative PT of $16.8/share as things stand here.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (in italics).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators, and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this, and a valuation target of €140/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's not attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of January 2026.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: ERC: Strong Long-Term Results For This Multi-Sector Bond Fund
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

The Allspring Multi-Sector Income Fund (ERC) provides investors with exposure to a well-rounded fixed-income portfolio in a closed-end fund wrapper. The fund provides exposure to both investment-grade and below-investment-grade allocations, making it more balanced, but it also incorporates leverage, which adds risk and increases volatility.

This is not a fund I've covered previously, at least not a focus article, but it has provided a solid long-term track record of results. It's also trading at a decent discount to its net asset value, though not at a bargain price, as we'll explore; it also isn't looking overly expensive. Easing rates should continue to benefit this fund as well, as short-term rates decline and their borrowing costs ease. If longer-term rates decline or remain relatively stable as they have been, then ERC looks like an attractive alternative for income-focused investors.

ERC Basics

  • 1-Year Z-score: -1.31
  • Discount/Premium: -6.59%
  • Distribution Yield: 9.33%
  • Expense Ratio: 1%
  • Leverage: 29.6%
  • Managed Assets: $402.3 million
  • Structure: Perpetual

ERC's investment objective is “to seek a high level of current income consistent with limiting its overall exposure to domestic interest rate risk.”

To attempt to achieve this, the fund will:

Under normal market conditions, the fund allocates approximately 30%–70% of its total assets to a sleeve consisting of below-investment-grade (high yield) debt; approximately 10%–40% to a sleeve of foreign debt securities, including emerging market debt; and approximately 10%–30% to a sleeve of adjustable-rate and fixed-rate mortgage-backed securities and investment-grade corporate bonds

The fund is leveraged, and that carries additional costs due to the interest on the borrowings. That would push the fund's total expense ratio to 3.22% as of the last fiscal year end, 2025. Given that the Fed has been cutting its benchmark rate, and this fund pays borrowings based on the secured overnight funding rate plus a spread, those costs have been easing for the fund. They leave the door open to hedge rates with interest rate swaps or futures contracts; however, they had none outstanding as of the latest annual report.

Performance: Solid Long-Term Results

Given the investment-grade allocation within the fund, that increases the fund's interest rate sensitivity relative to funds that invest simply in an entirely high-yield portfolio. They list the average duration of the bond portfolio at 2.81 years. Combining that with the leverage the fund employs, we saw that 2022 resulted in a significant hit to the fund's total returns. In particular, the fund's total share price saw a sharp drop because the fund had begun to trade at a premium going into those years when the Fed raised their benchmark rates aggressively.

At this point, the fund's total returns over the last five years have matched similarly to those of the iShares iBoxx $ High Yield Corporate Bond ETF (HYG), but I've also included the iShares iBoxx $ Investment Grade Corporate Bond ETF (LQD) for some added context. The effective duration for HYG comes in at 2.77 years, and for LQD, it comes in at 7.98 years.

As we've seen, the Fed is now in a rate-cutting cycle with 75 basis points of cuts in late 2024 and another 75 basis point cuts at the tail end of 2025; the funds have been performing better. However, LQD still remains in the red, showing just how hard hit the higher-quality bonds were impacted by higher rates.

With that said, over the long term, ERC has been able to outperform both of these passively managed index-based ETFs. Given the positive returns during this period and ERC's leverage, this would be expected.

In the fund's latest annual report for the fiscal year ending Oct. 31, 2025, they also provide a comparison of performance to other benchmarks, which would reflect the more flexible approach of ERC. In this case, the fund has strongly outperformed these indexes as well.

In terms of valuation, ERC trades similar to its longer-term historical discount level. That generally means that the fund is neither looking too expensive, but it's also not looking like too much of a bargain either. On a shorter-term basis, we can make an argument that it is a bit cheaper—with the 5-year average discount level sitting around -4.25%. That would be thanks to seeing the fund flirt with a premium in late 2021 through around 2023.

Distribution: Managed Policy Based on NAV

The distribution is adjusted monthly, and that has been the case for many years now, going back to around 2016/2017.

That isn't unusual for the Allspring closed-end funds, as we've looked at Allspring Utilities and High Income Fund (ERH), which pays at an 8% NAV rate.

For ERC, they set the rate at 8.75% “based on the fund's average monthly net asset value per share over the prior 12 months.” This was previously 8%, and that's why investors would have seen a sizeable increase in November 2024. Thanks to the discount on the fund, investors can enjoy a 9.33% distribution rate.

To fund the distribution, the fund will require some capital appreciation to help compensate for a shortfall in net investment income coverage. NII coverage came in at ~82.6%. Despite lower rates, the fund saw its NII decline for the last year, but that can be from simply repositioning in the underlying portfolio, too.

For tax purposes, we see that the fund's payout is primarily classified as ordinary income, which is to be expected given the overwhelming allocation to fixed-income instruments. However, we also see some return of capital distributions. That can come from a shortfall in income generation and capital gains—but it can also be the result of capital loss carryforwards offsetting realized gains, too. In this case, ERC is carrying a fairly sizeable balance of capital loss carryforwards.

ERC's Portfolio

The fund listed the latest portfolio turnover coming in at 36.63%. In total, the fixed-income allocation is noted to come in at 98.30%, with cash and equivalents at 1.09% and equity at 0.62%. Really making it what we would consider a multi-sector fixed-income fund. The equity allocation is too small to really matter.

Within the portfolio, the fund is invested fairly heavily in the BB and B allocations, each of which comprises the largest percentage of the portfolio. However, it is fairly balanced out with a sizeable allocation to AAA as well.

The fund is mostly allocated to corporate bonds, but also allocations to Treasuries, securitized products, and sovereign debt.

ERC is also not restricted to the U.S. alone, with around 33% being allocated to other geographic regions. That can increase flexibility for the fund management team to invest where they feel is best.

That translates into most of the top holdings actually being debt issued by countries outside the U.S.

At the same time, we can see these allocations are quite small, with the fifth-largest holding already dropping below a 1% allocation. The fund lists 510 holdings in total, so it is significantly diversified. That also wouldn't consider the securitized products where underlying loans/debt obligations are pooled together, providing exposure to hundreds or thousands of other obligors.

Conclusion

ERC seems like a fairly solid multi-sector fixed-income fund. They pay a distribution rate based on 8.75% of the trailing 12-month NAV average. This is then adjusted with each monthly payout, something that some income-focused investors don't tend to like to see. However, it does make it predictable in terms of knowing where the payout is heading. If NAV is rising, the payout will start to trend higher eventually as well, or vice versa. The fund is trading around its longer-term historical discount level, but on a short-term basis, it appears like a bit better value. That could make it an interesting idea for income investors looking for a diversified fixed-income fund, but perhaps utilizing a dollar-cost average approach instead of jumping all in at once.

data.title: ZTS Stock: High Profitability Vs. Lagging Growth | 2-Minute Analysis
data.description:
Follow Zoetis on Seeking Alpha!

Learn more about Seeking Alpha Quant Ratings

Get Started With Seeking Alpha Premium Now

This video's transcript was generated by a third party. It is not curated or reviewed and is provided for convenience and information purposes only. The accuracy and completeness of the transcript are not guaranteed.

Welcome to 2 Minute Analysis. Our goal is to not only entertain, but provide value and insights about the investments you care about. Today's episode is from this request. So, let's throw 2 minutes on the clock and dive-in.

Today, we are looking at Zoetis Inc., ticker symbol ZTS. Kicking things off here with the Quant rating system, it is currently a Hold rating on the stock, and we're going to dive through some metrics here in just a moment.

Jumping over to the Seeking Alpha Analysts, they currently have a Hold in aggregate on the stock, and that's from five analysts providing coverage in the last 90-days. Lastly, Wall Street is a Buy on the stock and that's from 20 analysts providing coverage in the last 90-days. To learn more about how the Seeking Alpha Quant system and the Seeking Alpha Analysts outperform the market, visit the link in the description of this video.

So, let's dive deeper. This is a $54.92 billion market capitalization company found within the healthcare sector and the pharmaceuticals industry. Kicking things off with the Valuation is currently a D- grade, and we're going to look at the Enterprise Value-to-EBITDA here currently at 14.65, compared to the sector at 12.71 and feeling in-line there. But if we look at that PEG non-GAAP Forward, that is 2.58 compared to the sector at 1.76. Most would consider that a bit of over valuation.

The Growth grade is currently a D-. Year-over-year revenue growth of 2.68% is below the sector median at 6.38%. Also want to highlight that this is below the five-year average of 8.06% for this company.

Profitability is currently an A+ grade. Net income margin of 28.21% dramatically beats the sector of only about half of a full percentage point here. Nice profitability for Zoetis.

Momentum is a D+ grade. One year price performance is down 25.07%, compared to the sector, which is mainly flat. Not a good thing to see there.

Lastly, Revisions is a D+ with three up revisions and 12 down revisions for earnings per share over the last three months. And one up revision and 14 down revisions for revenue over the last three months.

Now, this is a dividend paying stock. It pays a yield of 1.70%, and the five year growth rate is 20.11%. Looking at the safety grade, it is an A, the growth grade is an A+. The yield is a C, and consistency is a B-. Jumping into consistency, they've been paying their dividend for 12 years here. Looking at dividend safety, looks like the cash flow is going to keep this dividend pretty stable. $2.92 billion of cash from operations here for Zoetis. So, go ahead and follow Zoetis here on Seeking Alpha to get free breaking news alerts. The link is found beneath this video, and that's going to wrap it up for this episode.

If you have a ticker you want us to cover, add it to the comment section below, and don't forget to follow Seeking Alpha so you get notified when the next 2 Minute Analysis gets published.

Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Content provided for informational purposes only. Seeking Alpha does not offer any personalized investment advice and is not a licensed securities dealer, broker, US investment adviser, or investment bank.

data.title: XPH: Healthcare Dashboard For January
data.description:
This monthly article series offers a top-down analysis of the healthcare sector based on value, quality, and momentum metrics. It may also help analyze ETFs like The Health Care Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (XLV), whose largest holdings are used to calculate these metrics. This article will focus on a subsector ETF: State Street SPDR S&P Pharmaceuticals ETF (XPH).

Shortcut

The next two paragraphs in italics describe the dashboard methodology. They are necessary for new readers to understand the metrics. If you are used to this series or if you are short of time, you can skip them and go to the charts.

Base Metrics

I calculate the median value of five fundamental ratios for each industry: Earnings Yield (“EY”), Sales Yield (“SY”), Free Cash Flow Yield (“FY”), Return on Equity (“ROE”), Gross Margin (“GM”). The reference universe includes large companies in the U.S. stock market. The five base metrics are calculated on the trailing 12 months. For all of them, higher is better. EY, SY and FY are medians of the inverse of Price/Earnings, Price/Sales, and Price/Free Cash Flow. They are better for statistical studies than price-to-something ratios, which are unusable or non-available when the “something” is close to zero or negative (for example, companies with negative earnings). I also look at two momentum metrics for each group: the median monthly return (RetM) and the median annual return (RetY).

I prefer medians to averages because a median splits a set into a good half and a bad half. A capital-weighted average is skewed by extreme values and the largest companies. My metrics are designed for stock-picking rather than index investing.

Value and Quality Scores

I calculate historical baselines for all metrics. They are noted respectively as EYh, SYh, FYh, ROEh, GMh, and they are calculated as the averages on a look-back period of 11 years. For example, the value of EYh for healthcare providers in the table below is the 11-year average of the median earnings yield in this industry.

The Value Score (“VS”) is defined as the average difference in % between the three valuation ratios (EY, SY, FY) and their baselines (EYh, SYh, FYh). The same way, the Quality Score (“QS”) is the average difference between the two quality ratios (ROE, GM) and their baselines (ROEh, GMh).

The scores are in percentage points. VS may be interpreted as the percentage of undervaluation or overvaluation relative to the baseline (positive is good, negative is bad). This interpretation must be taken with caution: the baseline is an arbitrary reference, not a supposed fair value. The formula assumes that the three valuation metrics are of equal importance.

Value and Quality chart

The next chart plots the Value and Quality Scores by subsector (higher is better).

Evolution since last month

The value score has deteriorated in pharmaceuticals & biotechnology and life science tools.

Momentum

The next chart plots median returns by subsector.

Interpretation

The healthcare providers industry is undervalued by about 19% relative to 11-year averages. Pharmaceuticals/biotechnology and healthcare equipment are close to their historical baselines in valuation. The former also shows a good quality score. Life science tools is the least attractive subsector, with the lowest value and quality scores.

Focus on XPH

State Street SPDR S&P Pharmaceuticals ETF was launched on 06/19/2006 and tracks the S&P® Pharmaceuticals Select Industry® Index. XPH has a portfolio of 59 stocks and an expense ratio of 0.35%, while the broad sector benchmark XLV charges only 0.08%.

XPH has a modified equal-weight methodology resulting in low company-specific risk. The top 10 holdings, listed below, represent 22.3% of asset value, and the largest position weighs 2.65%.

In fact, XPH has mostly gone sideways for over a decade. It dropped in 2015 and has never fully recovered, unlike the capital-weighted fund iShares U.S. Pharmaceuticals ETF (IHE), as plotted on the chart below.

In summary, XPH offers modified equal-weighted exposure to the pharmaceutical industry. It has lower company risk than capital-weighted ETFs in the sector and industry, but also significantly lower historical returns. XPH is not a compelling long-term holding and is best used in swing trading or in tactical allocation strategies involving an array of industry ETFs.

Dashboard List

I use the first table to calculate value and quality scores. It may also be used in a stock-picking process to check how companies stand among their peers. For example, the EY column tells us that a large healthcare provider company with an earnings yield above 0.0489 (or price/earnings below 20.45) is in the better half of the industry regarding this metric. A dashboard list is sent every month to Quantitative Risk & Value subscribers with the most profitable companies standing in the better half among their peers regarding the three valuation metrics at the same time. The stocks below are part of the list sent to subscribers a few weeks ago.

OGN

Organon & Co.

HRMY

Harmony Biosciences Holdings, Inc.

ALKS

Alkermes plc

INCY

Incyte Corp.

ILMN

Illumina, Inc.

IQV

IQVIA Holdings Inc.

HAE

Haemonetics Corp.

THC

Tenet Healthcare Corp.

UHS

Universal Health Services, Inc.

CON

Concentra Group Holdings Parent, Inc.

It is part of a rotational model with a positive bias toward the long term, not the result of in-depth stock analysis.

data.title: Celsius: The Comeback Is Just Getting Started
data.description:
Celsius Holdings (CELH) is far off its March 2024 all-time high near $100 but has also come a long way from its February 2025 low near $21, now sitting somewhere in the middle at $53.53 following its latest multi-week bounce. So, what's the move now? I believe CELH stock is a Buy. It looks like Celsius is being aggressive in the right ways, as it turned its business momentum around through acquisitions and strategic deals—like the ones regarding Alani Nu, Rockstar Energy, and PepsiCo (PEP)—while maintaining a healthy balance sheet and cash flows.

Although Rockstar is a declining brand, there are advantages to Celsius' acquisition of the brand in the U.S. and Canada. Further, management will try to stabilize Rockstar and believes it can improve its margins over time.

Looking at Celsius as a whole, I see a cash-flow positive company with plenty of growth ahead in a growing energy drink market. It's a company that's not afraid to take risks, and I like that. Valuation-wise, it's not cheap, but it's still reasonable when compared to Monster (MNST). Here's the caveat: while I rate CELH as a Buy, I wouldn't get too aggressive right now since it has rallied substantially recently, so it may be wise to wait for a dip to take a full position.

Risk-On Markets Like Growing Risk Takers, And CELH Fits The Description

When investing, it's important to take a step back and realize what actually moves stocks. The current risk-on market has been rewarding growth stocks that aren't sitting on their hands, often disregarding valuations. Last year, when Celsius' growth was slowing, the stock was falling like a rock. Celsius could have just played it safe, kept its cash pile, and kept growing its core brand at a relatively slow rate. That likely wouldn't have been very harmful from a business perspective, but it's not always optimal for stock price gains.

Celsius' Acquisitions Are Improving The Company

Instead, Celsius came out and announced the Alani Nu acquisition on February 20, 2025, for $1.8 billion. I know this is old news, but I'm mentioning it to show what actually moves a stock. You need catalysts. The acquisition caused the stock to bottom, and it's been trending higher ever since because Alani Nu turned CELH into a growth machine (while maintaining profitability) again.

Today, we still have the growth benefits and catalysts from the Alani Nu acquisition, which should support the stock price, but we also have the Rockstar Energy deal from August. Although Rockstar is a declining brand that PepsiCo bought in 2020 for $3.85 billion and offloaded to Celsius for $585 million (particularly, the deal allowed PepsiCo to acquire $585 million in “newly issued convertible 5% preferred stock”), it comes with benefits.

Besides the top-line boost of around $250M+ annually from Rockstar Energy, the more important part is that it strengthened Celsius' relationship with PepsiCo, as PepsiCo now has an 11% stake in Celsius following the deal (on an as-converted basis) and isn't competing with Celsius in the energy drink market anymore. Their interests are more aligned, and Celsius became PepsiCo's “strategic energy drink captain in the U.S.”

The strengthened relations are evident when you see that Alani Nu is being integrated into the PepsiCo distribution system. During the Morgan Stanley Global Consumer & Retail Conference 2025 on December 3, 2025, CFO Jarrod Langhans stated that a high-80s percentage of Alani's direct store delivery system had moved into Pepsi's system. Here's the quote:

Yes. I'd say for starters, getting 80% of the Alani DSD system in the U.S. into Pepsi as of 12/1 is spot on. We were hoping for that plus or minus. I do actually -- a couple of hours ago, I got a note saying it was actually -- as we got through this week, we were able to bring some more on. So it's probably high 80s as opposed to just kind of in excess of 80%. So we're really on track and doing a very good job. And what that means is the percentage of DSD that existed within the Alani system 80-plus percent or high 80s has now moved into the Pepsi system and Pepsi is delivering to that high 80% from a DSD perspective. We'll still see some more. You never get the full 100, but we're on track to get even more beyond that high 80s number. So really excited about that.

In other words, this will help grow Alani Nu's reach, and the company mentioned that most of the financial benefit is expected this quarter. JPMorgan analyst Andrea Teixeira put it this way a couple of weeks ago:

While Alani Nu growth has slowed into the end of the year, we believe there is a long growth runway for the brand as it transitions most of its volume to the PepsiCo (PEP) distribution system in the U.S., which should allow for greater breadth/depth of distribution in the important convenience store channel and also foodservice (minimal presence today).

Besides, despite Rockstar being a lower-margin business, CFO Langhans expects its margins to improve starting in the first half of this year as the company integrates sourcing and production.

Therefore, CELH is taking risks to improve growth, but they're calculated risks, and the market has been sending shares higher as a result. As someone who has been watching from the sidelines, I see a competent management team that is doing things that work in the current market environment to send shares higher, supporting my bullish thesis.

Celsius Has Strong Cash Flows

A lot of the things I mentioned above would be much less important if Celsius was bleeding cash, but its cash flows are healthy, with $123.8 million of FCF in 2023, $239.5 million in 2024, and $523.6 million for the trailing 12 months.

For fiscal 2025 and 2026, the consensus of five analysts suggests $402.1 million and $428.9 million in FCF, respectively (per simplywall.st). Based on the consensus of four analysts, FCF is forecast at $504.54 million for 2027.

And this high free cash flow doesn't come with high amounts of stock-based compensation, as TTM SBC sits at just $24.75 million.

The Company's Debt Levels Are Manageable

As of the end of Q3, Celsius had $870.47 million in debt and $805.96 million in cash and equivalents on its balance sheet. However, that's a bit dated. After the quarter ended, management paid down $200 million of its debt using its cash on hand, and its total debt fell to “roughly $700 million,” per Langhans. The company also reduced its term note by 75 basis points, reducing its annual interest rate expense by ~$20 million starting from this year.

Anyway, my point is that CELH has plenty of cash to offset its debt position, and it has the free cash flow to make debt payments. Again, this is responsible risk-taking.

The Valuation Isn't Cheap But It's Reasonable

While I don't think this is a “valuation-driven” market, it's still worth looking at the valuation based on forward estimates. We can see that analysts expect EPS of $1.47 for 2026 (reflecting the acquisition growth) and EPS of $1.80 for 2027, leading to forward P/E of 36.12x and 29.55x for 2026 and 2027, respectively.

I'd argue that given Celsius' growth rate and potential, the valuation isn't that high. It's just not cheap. Revenue estimates also show relatively strong growth, with 31.8% growth expected in 2026 and 11.4% expected the following year, giving it FWD price/sales ratios of 4.25x and 3.8x, respectively.

At the same time, Monster, its main competitor, trades at a higher valuation despite lower growth. CELH and MNST actually have similar forward P/E ratios for 2027, with Monster's at 30.8x, although CELH's expected growth rate is almost 2x higher for that year.

Even MNST's forward price/sales ratios of 8.5x for 2026 and 7.8x for 2027 are about double CELH's forward price/sales despite lower expected growth and despite them having similar gross margin profiles (52.4% TTM gross margin for CELH and 55.8% for MNST).

Analyzed in this way, CELH stock's valuation looks reasonable.

The Risks To Consider

The first risk to consider is the fact that the energy drink market is highly competitive and can change quickly. For example, Rockstar Energy used to be one of the top energy drink companies in the 2000s. Now, it's quite low in the rankings, with a 2.4% share of Q3-2025 “MULO+ W/C retail dollar sales.”

Celsius needs to be on top of its game because energy drinks have a higher chance of becoming “fads” that fade out if the brand can't adapt to changing consumer needs. But at least the CELH portfolio contains 3 different brands now, so if one underperforms, the other could potentially make up for it.

Another thing to consider is that growth expectations are high, and while the valuation looks reasonable compared to MNST stock, we also need to factor in that MNST is able to command a higher valuation because it's more stable and predictable, with a longer track record. If you're seeking stability in this industry, go for MNST. But if you're comfortable risking more and betting on growth, go with CELH.

Next, while I believe the stock isn't in a downtrend anymore, the recent bounce has been strong and has lasted several weeks, increasing the chance of a short-term pullback. I wouldn't aggressively buy at current prices. Nonetheless, it's a good sign seeing the higher highs and higher lows since the bottom in February 2025.

The Bottom Line On CELH Stock

CELH stock has several things going for it, leading me to a Buy rating. I believe it should head higher over the next few quarters due to its high growth and its management team that isn't afraid to take calculated risks. In this market, high growth and risk-taking are what's being rewarded. The stock has come out of its downtrend and is set to grow nicely while maintaining high cash flows. The risks are there, but the rewards outweigh them. I'd just be careful about taking a full position in the stock after the recent rally.

data.title: REMX: Big Policy Tailwinds, Bigger Volatility
data.description:
Thesis

I wrote about the VanEck Rare Earth and Strategic Metals ETF (REMX) last year as a broad, educational tour of the fund, not a buy or sell call. REMX, at its core, is a pretty concentrated vehicle. It owns a narrow group of miners, refiners, and recycling companies spread across the globe, all tied in some way to rare earth elements and other materials. The fund is meant to follow the MVIS Global Rare Earth Strategic Metals Index. While that name sounds precise and technical, the companies behind it are real businesses. Many of them operate in politically unstable regions, have inconsistent profitability, and often need many years to develop before generating results.

That said, this investment theme has done extremely well recently. REMX is up about 109% over the past year, compared with roughly 17% for the S&P 500 (SP500). So far this year, REMX is up more than 20%, while the index is barely positive.

Given that, after reviewing the latest headlines about a bipartisan group on Capitol Hill floating a new agency armed with $2.5 billion to jump-start rare earths and other critical minerals, I’m taking a neutral view on REMX.

REMX and Government Intervention

According to Reuters, the bipartisan bill would set up a board with the authority to buy, store, and sell critical minerals, including rare earths, lithium, and nickel. The goal is to steady prices and reduce China’s ability to flood the market with supply in order to put financial pressure on projects outside China. For mineral producers located outside China, having a reliable, long-term buyer could reduce the risk of extreme price drops. That reduced risk is important because fear of large losses has made it harder to secure financing for expensive, capital-intensive mining projects.

To me, the bigger picture is really about working together with allies, not the reserve itself. Reuters has also been writing about the U.S. pushing the G7 and other partners to move faster on reducing reliance on China for critical minerals. If this reserve is part of a larger plan where allied governments and possibly large industrial buyers commit to actually buying the product, that matters much more. $2.5 billion by itself is not enough to create an entirely new supply chain. It can unstick some wheels. It can’t replace the wheel.

And, mind you, REMX isn’t exactly neatly aligned with the stated policy goal. The ETF has non-trivial exposure to Chinese issuers.

Those companies can benefit from higher realized prices, sure. They’re also part of the ecosystem Washington is explicitly trying to reduce dependence on. That makes REMX kind of awkward. Not uninvestable. This makes REMX less of an all-or-nothing investment compared to a portfolio that excludes China entirely. However, REMX can still underperform if successful reshoring causes market share and profit margins to move away from established companies that are based in China.

With all of that in mind, among the largest holdings, MP Materials (MP) stands out as the most obvious U.S.-based company that could benefit if the reserve becomes a reliable, long-term buyer of NdPr oxide and related products. I’m somewhat sympathetic to the bull case there. If there's a system that buys material when prices are weak, it could lower the risk that MP Materials has to slow down or pause its production ramp every time market prices turn unfavorable.

That said, a reserve does not eliminate real-world operating limits. MP Materials’ Q3 2025 earnings release explained that separated NdPr production has been ramping over the past year and that sales of magnetic precursor products have become a meaningful source of revenue instead of having almost no revenue in the prior year. This matches management’s strategy to move further downstream. Good. A reserve that purchases materials when prices are low could help prevent production from constantly stopping and restarting, but it wouldn't remove the need to qualify products, improve manufacturing yields, or deal with cost pressures as production volumes increase.

For me, the closest analogue is Lynas Rare Earths (LYSCF). The strategic value is clear. But processing requires reliable energy, chemical reagents, proper waste handling, and a steady supply of raw material to meet product specifications, and high fixed costs quickly hurt results if operations fall behind.

Another important economic factor is that defense needs are expanding what the government now considers “critical” materials. And this makes it more likely that the government will stay financially involved across many different raw materials for a long time. The Financial Times reported that the Pentagon has invested $150 million in a U.S. gallium project to reduce reliance on China for refined gallium, which is used in advanced military electronics. The report also warned that China can still try to protect its market position by lowering prices to make it harder for new suppliers to compete.

But even if all of that is true, REMX can still end up trading like a lithium product in the near term. Lithium is a critical mineral, not a rare earth element. That’s another headache. The ETF’s lithium exposure is big enough that lithium pricing can dominate performance regardless of what Washington is doing on rare earths. Recent coverage has pointed to a sharp January rebound in lithium pricing and renewed investor interest in Albemarle (ALB), tied to energy storage demand as well as EVs.

REMX and the Cost of Exposure

Based on Morningstar style metrics, REMX looks expensive compared to other funds in the Natural Resources category across all the usual valuation measures. REMX's P/E ratio is 31.73, while the category average is 16.35; meanwhile, its P/S ratio is 2.70, compared with 1.52 for the category. And its price-to-cash-flow ratio is 20.82, versus 9.50 for similar Natural Resources funds.

The fund shows long-term earnings growth of about 64%, and at first glance, that looks strong. But when you slow down and look closer, it feels inflated. Sales are actually down 3%, and cash flow is down more than 36%. Moreover, the standard deviation is about 35%, and annualized volatility runs over 42%. Both are extreme versus the ETF median.

The tracking error is around 33% over one, three, and five years. Basically, that means paying premium multiples is really a take on a very specific mix of factors and a specific view of the supply chain. So if that supply-chain view turns out to be wrong, there's no meaningful diversification cushion to protect you, the investor.

Also, you’re not getting paid to wait. The income just isn’t there. Because of that, this really comes down to total return, and specifically to price going up. Now, the 0.58% expense ratio isn’t crazy on its own. Still, it hurts more when cash flow growth is rolling over and volatility is elevated.

Final Takeaway

All things considered, I’d hold it for now. I think there could be more real upside for REMX if the reserve and related offtake agreements reduce supply pressure and keep projects funded. If that happens, prices could stay strong. That said, I think the downside is steeper if prices fall or if China causes another supply glut. This is really only for investors who can handle risk. Even then, I’d keep your position sizes small or treat it as a trade around catalysts. I’d avoid it entirely if you’re looking for income, stability, or broad diversification.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: State Street: Elevated Markets Continue To Boost Fees
data.description:
Investors had much to cheer in 2025, as global stock markets recorded another year of impressive gains. Buoyant markets have also improved the fortunes of custody bank State Street (STT), which saw its adjusted net income rise 13% last year to nearly $3 billion.

Covering the shares in November, I did express reservations about this. Valuation was the main one, as State Street's cycle-high profitability had also won it a richer valuation from the market. As you can see below, this has been a nice tailwind for the stock, though it makes the investment case trickier for prospective investors. I was also concerned that State Street had more riding on higher asset levels than its closest peer, Bank of New York Mellon (BK). While the latter has also benefited from strong markets, its growth has also had a broader base.

With fresh financials to digest, now is a good time to review things. I remain cautious, with State Street's valuation now running well beyond its recent historical norms.

Buoyant Fee Revenue

State Street recently released its Q4 2025 results, posting $845 million in adjusted net income on revenue of $3.67 billion. Revenue rose 7.5% year-on-year, while adjusted net income grew 240 basis points ahead of this. State Street's full-year results were also strong, with revenue and net income rising 7.4% and 13.5%, respectively.

Strong stock markets have been a key driver of this performance. State Street generates around 40% of its revenue from asset servicing fees, which include its core custody and administrative services. Much of this revenue is billed directly against assets under custody and/or administrative ('AUC/A'), meaning changes to market levels directly influence this line. State Street reported $53.8 trillion in AUC/A last quarter, with equities accounting for roughly 60% of that figure. Based on start-of-period balances, AUC/A grew 10.5% year-on-year, while servicing fees increased 8%, hinting at ongoing margin pressure in this business.

Of course, State Street isn't just a custody bank. It is also an investment manager, reporting over $5.6 trillion in assets under management ('AUM') last quarter. Many readers will be familiar with the SPDR range of exchange-traded funds, which are included in this business.

Equities account for over 60% of State Street's AUM, so rising markets have also been beneficial here. On top of this, State Street is also seeing positive net flows. The fact that passive funds account for around 90% of its AUM has helped, as these products have been taking share from active solutions for years. As a result, State Street saw management fees rise 15% last quarter to $662 million.

Crucially, fee growth is outpacing State Street's costs. Combined, State Street generated $2.05 billion in servicing and management fees last quarter, up 10.3% year-on-year. Meanwhile, underlying operating expenses rose 6.4%. This high degree of operating leverage is why the company's earnings growth has been so strong recently.

Also, because this growth is coming from capital-light business, it is having a positive impact on State Street's profitability. The company earned a 21.5% return on tangible common equity ('ROTCE') last quarter, which, as you can see above, is well above its ten-year average.

Better Interest Trends

Having been a concern of mine last time, interest revenue was better last quarter. Net interest income rose 7% year-on-year to $802 million, with State Street's net interest margin expanding 14 basis points sequentially to 1.1%. This brings its NIM back in line with its ten-year average.

Like other banks, State Street takes deposits and invests them in interest-earning assets. In contrast to other banks, its earning assets skew heavily to cash and investment securities rather than loans. On the liability side, it has been able to lower funding costs, given a fall in short-term interest rates.

At the same time, it isn't losing as much on its assets. Yields on cash and loans have fallen, but State Street has around $40 billion in held-to-maturity securities. These were only yielding 2.19% last quarter, well below prevailing market rates. The ongoing repricing of this part of its asset base should be a multi-year tailwind, so I expect more NIM gains from here. A steeper yield curve will naturally help, and this may also explain why its shares have done so well recently.

Attractive Capital Returns

Because its profitability is so strong right now, State Street can comfortably fund a high shareholder payout while maintaining ample capital. The company did $635 million in capital returns last quarter, over 90% of its net income. This took its full-year distributions to around $2.1 billion, for a 78% total payout ratio. The buyback component is obviously providing an extra boost to share-level metrics (adjusted EPS growth was around 530 basis points higher than adjusted net income growth last year).

Despite spending heavily on dividends and buybacks, State Street's capital cushion has only increased. It was sitting on an 11.7% Common Equity Tier 1 Ratio at the end of last quarter, up 80 basis points year-on-year, leaving it 120 basis points over the mid-point of management's 10%-11% target range. Alongside its healthy ROTCE, this surplus should allow for attractive capital returns in 2026.

Valuation Concerns

Like its business, State Street's stock has also been a strong performer, returning around 40% over the past year. This partly reflects the growth of its business, but valuation uplift has also contributed. For instance, State Street currently trades for $132.41, which is equal to 2.36 times its tangible book value. That's up from roughly 2.05 a year ago, and it's around 30% above its recent historical average.

Relative to past norms, State Street's trailing price-to-earnings ratio is also on the expensive side. The company earned $10.30 per share in adjusted net income last year, for a multiple of almost 13. That compares to a recent historical average of roughly 11. Moreover, State Street's earnings are currently benefiting from a cyclically strong ROTCE. Using its historical average ROTCE (around 17%), its P/E ratio would rise to nearly 15.

Now, I am not suggesting that we will see a reversion to this average in the near term. However, it is important to appreciate that elevated stock markets are supporting State Street's profitability. In turn, equities are themselves benefiting from historically high valuations, as shown below.

For that reason, I prefer to look at book value-based valuations as a guide for whether State Street is reasonably valued or not. Since this would suggest its shares are trading at a premium valuation, I remain cautious. Accordingly, I continue to rate the shares “Hold.”

data.title: Alibaba Qwen Upgrade: The Market Can No Longer Ignore This AI Catalyst
data.description:
It was a highly anticipated AI launch in the China AI scene. The biggest of China's cloud companies, Alibaba Group Holding Limited (BABA), is trying to recapture the glory days of its pre-tech crackdown preeminence, while also fending off the significant challenges from PDD Holdings (PDD) and also one of the world's most valuable startups, ByteDance (BDNCE). That competition has spilled over to the AI arena, as China's intensely competitive battleground requires a never-ending pursuit of success, or risk getting wiped out by arch rivals who are always fervently creeping up their shoulders.

Therefore, Alibaba's heavily publicized event in the Chinese media is billed as "a major upgrade to its flagship consumer-facing AI application, Qwen App." That already follows a series of significant launches that have undoubtedly bolstered Alibaba's position as one of the frontrunners in China's AI hierarchy. The market reaction this week has been highly positive so far, with BABA stock already consolidated above the $145 lows, as it surged confidently to retest the early November highs, as the market has lent its weight in concurrence with the significance of the upgrade to Qwen.

While there's no direct comparison to infer a resemblance of Alibaba's ecosystem moat in the U.S., I believe it reminds me somewhat of the Google renaissance (GOOGL, GOOG) that has percolated across Silicon Valley. As a reminder, Google owns the full stack, from custom chips to distribution. And that stranglehold across the ecosystem has afforded the Mountain View company to strengthen its AI prowess, while it closes in on OpenAI's (OPENAI) much vaunted leadership.

In my previous Alibaba write-up, I noted that while the stock's valuation has mean-reverted, I don't think the market has fully revalued BABA's AI narrative. And the recent event goes to show that my conviction isn't without a valid basis, as the efforts are designed to leverage on its ecosystem moat, similar to how Google has astutely made Gemini a force to be reckoned with among its leading AI peers right now.

As for Alibaba, I believe the Hangzhou-headquartered company realized that if there's a moment to finally capitalize on its ecosystem moat to seize the domestic AI narrative now, it couldn't have arrived at a more opportune time. And I believe the focal points in China's overarching AI strategy truly play into Alibaba's underlying strengths, right? China appears to be racing ahead into applied AI.

I believe the world's second-largest economy and unquestionably America's most significant AI rival, is seeking to achieve a decisive breakthrough in embodied AI, ahead of its U.S. peers. The recent CES at Las Vegas was a showcase of AI's ability to integrate its software and hardware prowess, as the demonstrations show their pace in rapidly infusing "artificial intelligence into real-world products." It also aligns with the forecasted 18% CAGR of China's AI hardware market through the rest of the decade. Notably, it could very well cement China's position as the world's preeminent player in the realm of physical AI.

Hence, there's little wonder that Alibaba's latest foray seeks to proliferate AI across its whole ecosystem, that also entails a "deep integration of core services." And the efforts are closely aligned with China's AI strategy of making artificial intelligence practical and user-friendly, potentially pivotal to rapidly diffusing AI across the economy.

Here's the big deal for China AI investors. Because we should no longer view Qwen as a "standalone" or disparate app from Alibaba's business model. Instead, we now have the suite of "Taobao, Taobao Instant Commerce, Alipay, Fliggy, and Amap" being consolidated into what Alibaba believes is a "unified AI interface."

Hence, while China's most cutting-edge AI capabilities appear to still be falling behind its American peers, it has always endeavored to successfully catch up. And the level of AI CapEx is still a fraction of what America's largest AI hyperscalers have committed, and are expected to spend even more through the rest of the decade. Thus, it does seem like Alibaba's RMB380B in CapEx commitment is seemingly much less significant. The U.S.'s ability to fence in China's AI compute capacities with export restrictions on AI chips and advanced lithography systems has created distinct advantages for the U.S. AI leaders. Yet, it still hasn't stymied China's efforts to narrow the gap in model capabilities, while also being capable to quickly deploying these capabilities into the applied AI space.

And if there's one company that could truly unleash applied AI, I believe it has got to be Alibaba. Given its e-commerce clout and tremendous influence and leadership in cloud computing, such an integration will arguably make Qwen an increasingly valuable interface. Its MAUs have also reportedly scaled to the 100M mark, with massive potential to accelerate and expand further. In other words, the best is yet to be, and Alibaba intends to race ahead before its peers could even catch their breath.

Also, Wall Street is increasingly confident that Alibaba Cloud will become an even more prominent growth driver through 2026. And I believe Qwen's integration and bolstered agentic capabilities will be put to the test, as MAUs and monetization opportunities will be closely watched, as these remain in their infancy.

Nevertheless, with Alibaba reportedly owning 40% of the cloud computing market share in China for 2025, these optionalities could see China's e-commerce behemoth garnering "80% of incremental industry revenue growth in 2026," potentially propelling Alibaba's market share to the 60% mark, a crucial milestone that should underpin its valuation re-rating.

Therefore, I'm getting the right price action signals in BABA's print that's shouting out on its uptrend continuation thesis. Observe BABA's distinct past pullbacks, bottoms, and then recovery in a familiar sequence. Such occurrences have duly recurred since April 2024, with buyers taking full advantage of steep pullbacks (including the one that bottomed in December) as opportunities to load up and ride the subsequent recovery.

BABA's forward earnings multiple of 24x is still markedly lower than the Magnificent Seven's 28x, and also Amazon's (AMZN) 32x P/E. Granted, they operate in different markets and also have substantive geopolitical considerations that could render such a direct juxtaposition less relevant than it seems. Yet, the premium over BABA's 10Y average of 20.4x doesn't suggest to me that the market has gone bonkers on its AI growth optionalities. And what does that mean? I think the BABA narrative has a lot more to run, even after the stellar >100% gains that the stock has managed over the past year.

Rating: Maintain Buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Thales S.A.: Solid Defense And Avionics Growth Solidify Margin Forecast
data.description:
Thales (THLEF) continues to see growth driven by solutions sales as opposed to aftermarket, deferring latent margin bumps from higher aftermarket contribution in the mix as discussed in our previous coverage. Higher-margin solutions such as avionics should continue to drive mix effects and margin, and additionally, the idea to merge their space business with some peers should also make space's contribution more robust, though as a J.V. Destocking trends within payments may continue, but sequential cyber and security improvement is expected. Thales may still have a relative case, but we think there are other ideas that continue to be more industrially levered to the defense spending boom while trading at similar valuations.

Results

The first thing to mention might be the merging of the space businesses with peers, in which Thales will have a 32.5% stake in a J.V.—expected to conclude after next year.

You discussed and I could elaborate just a bit on the synergies. We mentioned mid-triple-digit level of bottom line synergies 5 years after the closing.

Senior EVP of Finance, Pascal Bouchiat

That's quite considerable and amounts to about ~10% of the forecast revenues for the merged businesses, based on pooled R&D benefits and other redundancy eliminations.

Aerospace, overall in Q3, above our expectations in terms of level of growth in this business. By the way, not that much driven by aftermarket...

overall looking at our Aerospace segment end of September, we said 6.9% [in growth of sales]. And I explained that Avionics growth was significantly above this level and Space -- below Space in line with our expectations, meaning that Space end of September is around 2%.

Pascal Bouchiat

The other effect is the pickup in avionics in the mix, which had been driving the margin improvement in the H1. Forecast margin for the FY is expected to be around 100-300 bps higher for the FY, which is higher than the adjusted margin as of H1 at 12.1%.

Okay. So overall just to give you a rule of thumb, but probably a level of EBIT margin for the full year 2025, around 14% is probably what I have in mind. All of that being very much consistent with 12.2%, 12.4% overall at a group level.

Pascal Bouchiat

Solutions and not aftermarket are driving the growth, which could mean a latent effect from aftermarket could bolster margin as aftermarket tends to be higher revenue and usually lags solutions growth once the base of established solutions has grown.

The spending boom in European defense is being driven by laggards in military spending catching up to the more robust spenders in the quite independent France and the UK. Defense outpaced aerospace in terms of order intake growth, but comps will get tough for defense in Q4 due to a record quarter last year, and the order intake is generally lumpy. Orders in space haven't been booked yet related to SpaceRISE and could become booked next quarter or Q1 2026. Drones are also becoming more important in the mix at a triple-digit amount in sales, so maybe around 10% of sales when annualising H1 sales data.

In DIS, things are more troubled and performing below expectations on continued pressure on destocking of cards, not expected to go away imminently. Integration efforts from the Imperva acquisition are also weighing on sales due to sales force integrations. However, margins had been improving on cost and premiumisation initiatives such as those in Australia. The only growth area here is digital identity over the last 9 months.

Bottom Line

The drone exposure, which refers to loitering munitions, is pretty interesting, and aspects of it are going to be recurring due to the disposable nature of these military assets.

What we do is more to provide the equipment, the solutions, but also the system integration. So what does it mean? It means that in terms of specific solutions, we provide, of course, a set of different sensors. That's the first, the type of business we develop in this drone business. Second is what we call C2, command and control. So providing the intelligence to the systems. And third is military capabilities and in particular, through the development and the sales of what we call warhead in particular, to equipped at drones or loitering ammunitions.

Pascal Bouchiat

Other than that, growth has been good in civil and military applications. But there are other solid exposures at similar valuations that also have industrial scaling opportunities in munitions and rockets, such as BAE Systems (BAESY). On the other hand, Thales is cheaper than HENSOLDT (HAGHY). Industrial exposure is desirable given the high degree of certainty around the sales drivers. Thales has some of that in the sensor business to some extent, but munitions and rockets have more repeatable and disposable components and are more a focus in some competitors. We'll leave it lie for the moment, though if the synergies materialise for the underlying business within space, the effective multiple would start getting a discount of 10-15% from BAE.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Clear Secure: Beyond The Airport And The eGate Revolution
data.description:
ClearSecure (YOU) is a US-based digital identity company I have been following for over the past two years. My first coverage of the stock in 2024 rated the stock a buy, citing membership growth from strong travel demand as a key success factor.

Today, YOU is up over 70% since my first coverage, trading at over $33 per share as of late, surpassing my 1-year price target of $22.7 per share then by a significant margin.

Shares performance has been lackluster since going public in 2021, with a price return of merely -58% over that period. A lot of that also has to do with the 30% surge seen over the past year, despite the relatively muted YTD performance. YOU is currently down -1.8% on a YTD basis.

I maintain my buy rating with a 1-year price target of $38 per share, which is a potential upside of 13.6% from today’s level. I believe that YOU should benefit from a few catalysts into 2026, while risks remain minimal to moderate.

Financials Review

Fundamentals are solid overall. In Q3, YOU delivered $229.2 million of revenue, a 15.5% YoY growth. It appears that YOU has seen growth stabilizing from the 30% to 40% growth seen in previous years. Nonetheless, this is still a healthy growth, in my opinion, considering that the driver here is mainly the price increase for CLEAR+ to enhance ARPU.

Meanwhile, profitability appears to have improved. Net margin was 19.7% in Q3 2025, suggesting that YOU has maintained its profitability YoY. The same time last year, YOU delivered a 19% net margin as well. A significant improvement is seen in gross margin, which has currently been over 60% over the past two quarters, with Q3 2025’s 62% being a 5-year-high quarterly gross margin for the company. The biggest driver for this overperformance has been the e-gate automation that requires fewer human operations at the kiosks, reducing the overall cost per-member.

Balance sheet and liquidity remain robust, in my opinion. YOU ended Q3 with over $530 million of cash and short-term investments with zero debts, maintaining a relatively steady level over the past year. While operating cash flow (OCF) was negative for the quarter, it was merely due to the cyclicality around the partnership liability settlements. If we zoom out over a nine-month period ending Q3, YOU actually delivered $174 million of OCF, an over 9% YoY growth.

Catalyst

Overall, I believe that catalysts are relatively moderate to high for YOU in 2026, as there could be a few opportunities to unlock the management’s near-term growth and profitability guidance in FY 2025 and into 2026.

In my view, the first one is the wider deployment of automated eGates in 2026, which financially could help YOU realize operating leverage by reducing the number of human staff per station, as the management alluded to in earnings call:

This quarter, we delivered operating leverage across every line of our P&L, both, again, sequentially and year-over-year. And I think innovations such as eGates really gives us the opportunity to either reallocate capital and/or flow it through to the bottom line.

Source: Q3 earnings call.

As of Q3, YOU has made 10 deployments of these eGates, resulting in an upward trend in gross margins as of late. As such, I believe this will be the key for YOU to drive more expansion into the bottom line to achieve the management’s target of an above-30% adjusted EBITDA for the FY.

The second one, in my opinion, would be the Medicare deal, which will allow YOU to access a different market than the digital travel identity. Eventually, if the rollout proves to be successful in 2026, the narrative around investing in YOU becomes a play in universal digital identity instead of just travel, driving potential re-rating. Financially, the diversification should also help YOU reduce the cyclicality from the exposure to the travel industry.

Risk

Overall, risks are minimal to moderate. While the transition to eGates and the Medicare deal are both interesting growth stories for YOU, I believe there are some chances for setbacks due to implementation delays, which may result in YOU not realizing the savings.

Another area of concern that could be more of a longer-term risk for YOU is the notion of possible competition with TSA despite YOU serving as their partner, in my opinion. In light of the membership price increase last year in July. In general, the marginal benefit of YOU may continue to be tested if the technology of TSA PreCheck Touchless ID, which is the more affordable option, continues to get better over time.

As such, the Medicare deal could be quite critical for YOU, since it will prove to the market that its non-travel identity product is promising enough to offset any potential churn risk from the core business, driving a re-rating potential based on diversification strategy.

Valuation / Pricing

My 1-year target price for YOU is driven by the following assumptions for the bull vs. bear scenarios, based on consensus estimate. Accounting for all share classes conversion, I project around 132 million of shares outstanding:

  1. Bull scenario (70% probability) assumptions - YOU successfully expands outside the travel market, with the Medicare rollout seeing a huge success, consequently unlocking similar universal identity contracts with more government institutions. YOU is now valued as a universal identity as opposed to just a travel identity player. I assign YOU a P/S of 6x to reflect the premium rewarded by the market, a bit higher than where it is today at around 5x.

  2. Bear scenario (30% probability) assumptions - YOU’s Medicare implementation faces technical challenges, such as privacy-related issues or hardware malfunction, delaying growth and creating public scrutiny for the app. I assign YOU a forward P/S of 3x, near its 1-year low.

Consolidating all the information above into my model, I arrived at an FY 2026 weighted target price of $38 per share, suggesting a 13.6% potential upside from the current price level. I give the stock a buy rating. YOU appears to offer an attractive upside at the price point.

Conclusion

Having secured the Medicare contract, YOU is on the verge of transitioning from a digital travel identity to a more universal identity platform, presenting a re-rating potential. The scale up of eGates further presents an opportunity for a margin expansion into 2026. While implementation delay and competition from TSA’s Touchless ID could be risk factors, I believe that the reward from YOU’s expansion outside the travel identity market should outweigh the risk. I maintain my buy rating for the stock.
